entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
Misinformation, particularly in the context of blockchain technology and Bitcoin development, has been a significant issue. Examples of this are widespread across various platforms, ranging from social media to specialized forums. One notable instance involved an open-source project named Sapio, which was misrepresented in claims made on Twitter ([source](https://web.archive.org/web/20220503050140/https://twitter.com/coinableS/status/1521354192434073602)). Further misinformation included personal attacks aimed at the author of BIP 119, which contained inaccuracies that were propagated online ([source](https://nitter.net/s3cp256k1/status/1521238634111770624)).
The issue extends to respected figures in the field, such as Andreas Antonopoulos, who shared incorrect statements about CTV, a proposal linked to Bitcoin's protocol development. These inaccuracies were addressed by Ryan in a detailed email discussion ([source](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-May/020414.html)). Additionally, Michael Folkson was involved in spreading misleading information through a series of emails, which further complicated the discourse surrounding Bitcoin development ([sources](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-January/019728.html), [source](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-April/020235.html), [source](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-April/020286.html), [source](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-April/020343.html), [source](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-April/020386.html)).
Public figures like Peter Todd and Zac have also been noted for sharing misleading interpretations regarding BIP 119, its implications on Bitcoin and Layer-2 technologies. This controversy prompted responses aimed at clarifying these misunderstandings ([source](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-April/020322.html)). Misconceptions and outright falsehoods about BIP 119 were rampant on social media, as highlighted by influencers such as Peter McCormack, who admitted to not understanding BIP 119 but still labeled it negatively ([source](https://nitter.net/PeterMcCormack/status/1521253840963653632)). Insightful articles by Bitcoin Magazine attempted to demystify BIP 119 and analyze the surrounding controversy ([source](https://bitcoinmagazine.com/technical/what-is-bip-119-bitcoin-controversy-explained), [source](https://bitcoinmagazine.com/technical/analyzing-bip-119-and-the-controversy-surrounding-it)).
John Carvalho and others tweeted false statements related to BIP 119, contributing to the spread of misinformation. Their tweets can be found extensively documented online ([source](https://nitter.net/BitcoinErrorLog/status/1468599535538745359), [source](https://nitter.net/BitcoinErrorLog/status/1522652884218822658), [source](https://nitter.net/BitcoinErrorLog/status/1442554615967354880), [source](https://nitter.net/search?q=MIT%20(from%3ABitcoinErrorLog))). Furthermore, even when attempts were made to correct these misunderstandings, additional inaccuracies were sometimes introduced, as seen in Greg Maxwell’s Reddit comments ([source](https://www.reddit.com/r/Bitcoin/comments/uim560/bip_119/i7dhfpb/)).
This array of misinformation underscores the challenges faced by those involved in technical developments and highlights the need for more rigorous standards of review and communication within the community.
